core_thesis: K. C. Bhattacharyya (1875–1949) taught philosophy in the Bengal government colleges, directed the Indian Institute of Philosophy at Amalner (1933–35), and held the George V Professorship of Mental and Moral Philosophy at the University of Calcutta (1935–37); his method of "constructive interpretation" reworks classical Indian systems into systematic philosophy. Bhattacharyya explicitly frames Studies in Vedantism as philosophical construction on Vedantic lines, following the Upanishads, Brahma-sutras, Bhagavad-gita, and Shankara while confining itself to monistic interpretation ([Studies in Vedantism, Introduction](cite://kc-bhattacharyya/studies-in-vedantism/introduction)). His account of subjectivity distinguishes object as what is meant or referred to from the subject expressed by "I": the subject is not known as an object but is felt as free from objectivity ([The Subject as Freedom, The Notion of Subjectivity](cite://kc-bhattacharyya/studies-in-philosophy-vol-2/the-subject-as-freedom-the-notion-of-subjectivity)). He develops that freedom through bodily, psychical, and spiritual subjectivity, each with a corresponding range of objectivity, and treats spiritual psychology as a discipline for reversing the objective attitude ([The Subject as Freedom, The Notion of Subjectivity](cite://kc-bhattacharyya/studies-in-philosophy-vol-2/the-subject-as-freedom-the-notion-of-subjectivity)). In his Advaita essays, maya is neither real nor unreal, the jiva is Brahman wrongly taking itself as other, and Isvara is Brahman as Lord in relation to maya and the world ([Sankara's Doctrine of Maya](cite://kc-bhattacharyya/studies-in-vedantism/sankaras-doctrine-of-maya); [The Advaita and Its Spiritual Significance](cite://kc-bhattacharyya/studies-in-vedantism/the-advaita-and-its-spiritual-significance)). Moksha is not a produced state for him; sadhana is needed for the individual to realize the self's eternal freedom ([The Advaita and Its Spiritual Significance](cite://kc-bhattacharyya/studies-in-vedantism/the-advaita-and-its-spiritual-significance)).

cross_engagements.brief: Akalanka presses the same substrate structure axis in the metaphysical register, but with the value `many-coordinate-reals` rather than `one-self-standing`.
Abhinavagupta presses the same manifestation status axis in the metaphysical register, but with the value `expressive-manifestation` rather than `sublatable-not-null`.
Arthur Schopenhauer presses the same identity relation axis in the metaphysical register, but with the value `self-expression-or-appearance` rather than `non-otherness`.
Adi Sankara presses the same selfhood structure axis in the phenomenological register, but with the value `witness-self` rather than `relational-self`.
Anandabodha presses the same finite cognition axis in the epistemological register, but with the value `positive-ignorance` rather than `adhyasa-or-superimposition`.
Akshapada Gautama presses the same epistemic authority axis in the epistemological register, but with the value `plural-pramana-realism` rather than `no-single-authority`.
Anantakrishna Sastri presses the same truth-structure axis in the epistemological register, but with the value `hierarchical-standpoint-truth` rather than `alternative-irreducible-truths`.
Baladeva Vidyabhushana presses the same perspectivism axis in the epistemological register, but with the value `partial-perspectives-ranked` rather than `irreducible-true-perspectives`.
G. W. F. Hegel presses the same normativity axis in the ethical register, but with the value `ethical-life-in-institutions` rather than `self-legislating-subject`.
Bannanje Govindacharya presses the same practice-path axis in the soteriological register, but with the value `devotion-and-grace` rather than `knowledge-discipline`.
Alfred North Whitehead presses the same soteric end axis in the soteriological register, but with the value `not-soteriological` rather than `identity-with-ground`.
